LAW AND MOTION 302, "IN RESPONSE TO THE COVID-19 OUTBREAK, THE HEARING DATES FOR ALL LAW AND MOTION AND DISCOVERY MATTERS CURRENTLY CALENDARED IN DEPARTMENTS 301/302 ARE HEREBY VACATED. WHEN THE COURT RESUMES NORMAL OPERATIONS, THE PRESIDING JUDGE WILL ANNOUNCE PROCEDURES FOR SETTING HEARING DATES FOR THOSE CASES WHICH HAVE BEEN FULLY BRIEFED. OPPOSITIONS WILL BE DUE BASED ON THE RESCHEDULED HEARING DATE, BUT NOTHING PRECLUDES A PARTY FROM FILING AN OPPOSITION EARLIER." (302)

LAW AND MOTION 302, IN RESPONSE TO THE COVID-19 OUTBREAK, THE HEARING DATES FOR ALL LAW AND MOTION AND DISCOVERY MATTERS IN DEPARTMENT 302 FILED BEFORE JUNE 1, 2020 ARE OFF CALENDAR PURSUANT TO THE COURT'S GENERAL ORDERS. TO RE-SCHEDULE THE HEARING, PLEASE REFER TO THE PUBLIC NOTICE TO RESTORE SERVICES ISSUED ON MAY 27, 2020 AVAILABLE ON THE COURT'S WEBSITE AT HTTPS://SFSUPERIORCOURT.ORG/.

Notice Of Second Motion And Second Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement
Off Calendar Apr-12-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Kelsey Szamet, Esq. of Kingsley & Kingsley, APC (Tel: 818-990-8300) for plaintiff and the proposed class;
Atty. Steve Friedlander, Esq. of SV Employment Law Firm PC (Tel: 650-265-0222) for defendant Trackin, Co.

PLAINTIFF ADINA MEZA's Second Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement.

The matter is called, counsel appear to to schedule the final approval hearing.
DISCUSSIONS are held.

At the conclusion of discussions, the tentative ruling is adopted:

Plaintiff's second motion for preliminary approval of class action settlement is granted. The Court has reviewed the supplemental declaration of counsel, the amended class action settlement agreement and release of claims, and the revised class notice.

A declaration addressing the the changes to the notice and the funding timeline is to be filed by August 20, 2021.

The Final Approval Hearing is set for August 24, 2021 at 9:30 a.m. in Department 302.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)

CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Mar-29-2021 Continued To May-25-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Second Motion And Second Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement
Off Calendar Mar-19-2021 Continued To Apr-12-21 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Kelsey Szamet, Esq. of Kingsley & Kingsley, 818.990.8300, for Plaintiff Adina Meza;
Atty. Steve Friedlander, Esq. of SV Employment Law Firm P.C., 650.265.0222, for Defendant Trackin, Co.

Plaintiff ADINA MEZA's Second Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement.
Matter called, DISCUSSIONS are held.

Counsel appear to discuss the schedule for the continued hearing and to address the following issues as set forth in the Court's tentative ruling:

1. The proposed settlement agreement provides for funding of the total settlement amount over a period of three to four years ($10,000 every 90 days, beginning 30 days after final approval, subject to extensions for payments that fall due in the last quarter of a calendar year), and provides for distribution of the individual settlement payments only after the settlement is fully funded. (Section VII(9).) That material provision of the proposed agreement, which has an obvious bearing on the value to be attributed to the settlement, is not addressed in the moving papers. Nor is there any discussion of the need for such a provision or of the risk, in light of Defendant's financial condition, that the settlement will never be fully funded and therefore will never be effectuated. The Beretta Declaration states that at the time of the mediation in July 2019, Defendant "did not have significant assets, was not generating a profit, and was at risk of ceasing its business in light of the Action" (par. 5). There is no discussion, however, of Defendant's current financial condition or future prospects as they may bear on the proposed agreement.

2. Moreover, neither the proposed timing of the anticipated settlement payment nor the risk that Defendant will be unable to fully fund the settlement is disclosed in the proposed class notice. They must be. At a minimum, the notice must inform class members when they can anticipate receiving their share of the settlement, and must also inform class members that there is a risk that they will not receive their share of the settlement if Defendant goes out of business or becomes insolvent.

3. There are two other, more minor issues. First, the Court cannot find that notice to class members by first-class mail only is the best notice practicable. Notice must be provided both by first-class mail and by email, if available. Second, the settlement agreement should require the settlement administrator to establish a website where class members may access the pleadings, the proposed settlement agreement, and the class notice, as well as information regarding the fairness hearing.

At the conclusion of discussions, the matter is continued to April 12, 2021 at 9:30 a.m. in Department 302. The supplemental papers including the amendment to the proposed settlement agreement and the amended class notice are to be filed by April 5, 2021.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)

CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Aug-12-2020 Continued To Dec-01-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Class Action Settlement
Off Calendar Jul-27-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff's motion for preliminary approval of class action settlement and PAGA claim is denied without prejudice. While plaintiff provides an assessment of counsel's estimate of the putative class's maximum recovery (Szamet Decl. 46-58), the bases for the assessment are not clearly explained, and in some cases appear to be inconsistent or contradictory. For example, plaintiff asserts that through the data produced by defendant he determined the total tips received was $91,405.21 and contends that 60% of this amount, or $54,832.12, was unlawfully diverted from class members, but asserts without explanation that the tip deduction claim was valued at $31,219.74. Further, plaintiff states that the value of the PAGA claim was calculated at $900,000.00, but provides no explanation for how that figure was arrived at, or for how it was determined to allocate $20,000 of the $160,000 gross settlement amount to that component of the overall settlement. Further, while plaintiff asserts that one of the "main factors" affecting settlement was defendant's ability to pay a judgment and that defendant lacks sufficient financial resources to pay a larger settlement or judgment (Szamet Decl. 60-64), plaintiff provides no financial statements or other supporting information to support that conclusory assertion. More is required. (See, e.g., Haralson v. U.S. Aviation Services Corp. (N.D. Cal. 2019) 383 F.Supp.3d 959, 970-974.)

The court also has serious concerns about the provision of the proposed settlement agreement making class members responsible for any taxes and penalties assessed on the settlement amounts and binding them to indemnify defendant against any claims resulting from treatment of such payments as non-taxable damages and not subject to withholding or deduction for payroll and employment taxes. (Section VII(3), at pp. 11-12.) The court does not believe that provision is fair and reasonable to putative class members.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
